name: Fish Sauce (Nuoc Mam)
recipeInstructions:
Fish sauce is essential to Vietnamese cooking, comparable to salt in Western cuisine and soy sauce in Chinese cooking. It is used in almost every recipe. Produced from fresh anchovies and salt, layered in large wooden barrels, fish sauce production is a significant industry. Factories are located along the coast to ensure freshness of the fish being processed. Fermentation begins once a year during the fishing season. After about 3 months in the barrel, liquid drips from an open spigot and is poured back into the top. After approximately 6 months, the fish sauce is ready for use.
The first draining yields the highest quality fish sauce, which is lighter in color and perfectly clear. It is relatively expensive and reserved for table use. Subsequent drainings produce lower-quality fish sauce suitable for general cooking purposes. The towns of Phu Quoc and Phan Thiet are most renowned for their fish sauce production. Phu Quoc produces the finest fish sauce, some of which is exported. The "nhi" symbol on the label indicates the highest quality. If Vietnamese fish sauce is unavailable, Thai or Hong Kong varieties are acceptable. For table use, Squid Brand Fish Sauce is highly recommended; look for the "Ca Com" label, indicating that only anchovies were used, signifying the best quality for table use.
From "The Classic Cuisine of Vietnam," Bach Ngo and Gloria Zimmerman, Barron's, 1979.
Posted by Stephen Ceideberg; February 2 1992.
